**Child sexual abuse** (**CSA)** is a form of [child maltreatment](/wiki/Child_abuse "Child abuse") defined as any act that involves a child in, or exposes a child to, any type of [sexual activity](/wiki/Human_sexual_activity "Human sexual activity") that they are not developmentally prepared for, do not fully comprehend, do not have the capacity to [consent](/wiki/Consent "Consent") to, and which otherwise contravenes laws and societal taboos.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:02-3) Forms of child sexual abuse include engaging in [sexual behavior](/wiki/Sexual_behavior "Sexual behavior") with a child (whether by force, threats, coercion, manipulation, or other fraudulent means), [exhibitionism,](/wiki/Exhibitionism "Exhibitionism") [child grooming](/wiki/Child_grooming "Child grooming"), and using a child to produce, or expose them to [child sexual abuse and exploitation material](/wiki/Child_pornography "Child pornography") (CSAEM).[[3]](#cite_note-:02-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Williams_one2-4)

CSA is not confined to specific settings; it permeates various [institutions](/wiki/Institutional_abuse "Institutional abuse") and communities. CSA affects children in all [socioeconomic](/wiki/Socioeconomic "Socioeconomic") levels, across all racial, ethnic, and cultural groups, and in both rural and urban areas. In places where [child labor](/wiki/Child_labor "Child labor") is common, CSA is not restricted to one individual setting; it passes through a multitude of institutions and communities. This includes but is not limited to schools, homes, and online spaces where adolescents are exposed to abuse and exploitation. [Child marriage](/wiki/Child_marriage "Child marriage") is one of the main forms of child sexual abuse; [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF") has stated that child marriage "represents perhaps the most prevalent form of sexual abuse and exploitation of girls".[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) The effects of child sexual abuse can include [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"),[[6]](#cite_note-Roosa-6) [post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder"),[[7]](#cite_note-widom-7) [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety"),[[8]](#cite_note-levitan-8) [complex post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Complex_post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Complex post-traumatic stress disorder"),[[9]](#cite_note-9)[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and physical injury to the child, among other problems.[[10]](#cite_note-dinw-10) Sexual abuse by a family member is a form of [incest](/wiki/Incest "Incest") and can result in more serious and long-term [psychological trauma](/wiki/Psychological_trauma "Psychological trauma"), especially in the case of [parental incest](/wiki/Parental_incest "Parental incest").[[11]](#cite_note-Courtois-11)

Globally, nearly 1 in 8 girls experience sexual abuse before the age of 18.[[12]](#cite_note-Unicef-12) This means that over 370 million girls and women currently alive have experienced rape or sexual assault before turning 18.[[12]](#cite_note-Unicef-12) Boys and men are also affected, with estimates ranging from 240 to 310 million (about one in eleven) experiencing sexual violence during childhood.[[12]](#cite_note-Unicef-12) The prevalence of CSA varies across regions. Sub-Saharan Africa reports the highest rates, with 22% of girls and women affected, followed by Eastern and South-Eastern Asia.[[12]](#cite_note-Unicef-12)

Most sexual abuse offenders are acquainted with their victims; approximately 30% are relatives of the child, most often brothers, fathers, uncles, or cousins;[[13]](#cite_note-13)[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] around 60% are other acquaintances, such as "friends" of the family, babysitters, or neighbors; strangers are the offenders in approximately 10% of child sexual abuse cases.[[14]](#cite_note-Whealin-14) Most child sexual abuse is committed by men; studies on female child molesters show that women commit 14% to 40% of offenses reported against boys and 6% of offenses reported against girls.[[14]](#cite_note-Whealin-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Finkelhor1994-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The word *[pedophile](/wiki/Pedophile "Pedophile")* is commonly applied indiscriminately to anyone who sexually abuses a child,[[17]](#cite_note-ames-17) but child sexual offenders are not pedophiles unless they have a strong sexual interest in [prepubescent](/wiki/Prepubescent "Prepubescent") children.[[18]](#cite_note-barbaree-seto-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Oxford-19)[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Under the law, *child sexual abuse* is often used as an [umbrella term](/wiki/Umbrella_term "Umbrella term") describing criminal and civil offenses in which an adult engages in sexual activity with a [minor](/wiki/Minor_(law) "Minor (law)") or exploits a minor for the purpose of sexual gratification.[[20]](#cite_note-nspcc-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [American Psychological Association](/wiki/American_Psychological_Association "American Psychological Association") states that "children cannot consent to sexual activity with adults", and condemns any such action by an adult: "An adult who engages in sexual activity with a child is performing a criminal and immoral act which never can be considered normal or socially acceptable behavior."[[22]](#cite_note-delay-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Effects

### Psychological

Child sexual abuse can result in both short-term and long-term harm, including [psychopathology](/wiki/Psychopathology "Psychopathology") in later life.[[10]](#cite_note-dinw-10)[[24]](#cite_note-twin-24) Indicators and effects include [depression](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder"),[[6]](#cite_note-Roosa-6)[[25]](#cite_note-Widom2007-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Arnow-26) [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety"),[[8]](#cite_note-levitan-8) [eating disorders](/wiki/Eating_disorders "Eating disorders"),[[27]](#cite_note-Walsh2011-27) poor [self-esteem](/wiki/Self-esteem "Self-esteem"),[[27]](#cite_note-Walsh2011-27) [somatization](/wiki/Somatization "Somatization"),[[26]](#cite_note-Arnow-26) [sleep disturbances](/wiki/Sleep_disorder "Sleep disorder"),[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) and [dissociative](/wiki/Dissociative_identity_disorder "Dissociative identity disorder") and [anxiety disorders](/wiki/Anxiety_disorder "Anxiety disorder") including [post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder").[[7]](#cite_note-widom-7)[[30]](#cite_note-Treichel-30) While children may exhibit regressive behaviours such as [thumb sucking](/wiki/Thumb_sucking "Thumb sucking") or [bedwetting](/wiki/Nocturnal_enuresis "Nocturnal enuresis"), the strongest indicator of sexual abuse is sexual [acting out](/wiki/Acting_out "Acting out") and inappropriate sexual knowledge and interest.[[31]](#cite_note-APA2012-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Faller-32) Victims may withdraw from school and social activities[[31]](#cite_note-APA2012-31) and exhibit various learning and behavioural problems including [cruelty to animals](/wiki/Cruelty_to_animals "Cruelty to animals")[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36), [conduct disorder](/wiki/Conduct_disorder "Conduct disorder"), and [oppositional defiant disorder](/wiki/Oppositional_defiant_disorder "Oppositional defiant disorder") (ODD).[[27]](#cite_note-Walsh2011-27) [Teenage pregnancy](/wiki/Teenage_pregnancy "Teenage pregnancy") and risky sexual behaviors may appear in adolescence.[[37]](#cite_note-Tyler2002-37) Child sexual abuse victims report almost four times as many incidences of [self-inflicted harm](/wiki/Self-harm "Self-harm").[[38]](#cite_note-SelfHarm2003-38) Sexual assault among teenagers has been shown to lead to an increase in mental health problems, [social exclusion](/wiki/Social_exclusion "Social exclusion") and worse school performance.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/NIH_child_sex_abuse_disorders_graph.svg/500px-NIH_child_sex_abuse_disorders_graph.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NIH_child_sex_abuse_disorders_graph.svg)

A study funded by the US [National Institute of Drug Abuse](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Drug_Abuse "National Institute of Drug Abuse") found that "Among more than 1,400 adult females, childhood sexual abuse was associated with increased likelihood of drug dependence, alcohol dependence, and psychiatric disorders. The associations are expressed as [odds ratios](/wiki/Odds_ratio "Odds ratio"): for example, women who experienced nongenital sexual abuse in childhood were 2.83 times more likely to develop drug dependence as adults than were women who were not abused."[[41]](#cite_note-NIDA17-41)

CSA is associated with experiencing additional victimization in adolescence and adulthood.[[42]](#cite_note-Messman-Moore-42)[[43]](#cite_note-Polusny-43) Correlations have been found between childhood sexual abuse and various adult psychopathologies, including [crime](/wiki/Crime "Crime") and [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide"),[[14]](#cite_note-Whealin-14)[[44]](#cite_note-science-freyd-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Dozier-45)[[46]](#cite_note-Kendall-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-johnbriere-48) in addition to alcoholism and drug abuse.[[41]](#cite_note-NIDA17-41)[[43]](#cite_note-Polusny-43)[[49]](#cite_note-49) Males who were sexually abused as children more frequently appear in the criminal justice system than in a clinical mental health setting.[[31]](#cite_note-APA2012-31) A study comparing middle-aged women who were abused as children with non-abused counterparts found significantly higher health care costs for the former.[[26]](#cite_note-Arnow-26)[[50]](#cite_note-pmid18204885-50) Intergenerational effects have been noted, with the children of victims of child sexual abuse exhibiting more conduct problems, peer problems, and emotional problems than their peers.[[51]](#cite_note-Parenting2004-51)

A specific characteristic pattern of symptoms has not been identified,[[52]](#cite_note-Fergusson-52) and there are several hypotheses about the causality of these associations.[[6]](#cite_note-Roosa-6)[[53]](#cite_note-pmid11015813-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Studies have found that 51% to 79% of sexually abused children exhibit psychological symptoms.[[46]](#cite_note-Kendall-46)[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) The risk of harm is greater if the abuser is a relative, if the abuse involves intercourse or attempted intercourse, or if threats or force are used.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The level of harm may also be affected by various factors such as penetration, duration and frequency of abuse, and use of force.[[10]](#cite_note-dinw-10)[[24]](#cite_note-twin-24)[[60]](#cite_note-Beitchman-60)[[61]](#cite_note-pmid3704036-61) The [social stigma](/wiki/Social_stigma "Social stigma") of child sexual abuse may compound the psychological harm to children,[[61]](#cite_note-pmid3704036-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) and adverse outcomes are less likely for abused children who have supportive family environments.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

#### Post-traumatic stress disorder

Main articles: [Dissociation (psychology)](/wiki/Dissociation_(psychology) "Dissociation (psychology)") and [Post-traumatic stress disorder in children and adolescents](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder_in_children_and_adolescents "Post-traumatic stress disorder in children and adolescents")

[Child abuse](/wiki/Child_abuse "Child abuse"), including sexual abuse, especially chronic abuse starting at early ages, has been found to be related to the development of high levels of dissociative symptoms, which includes amnesia for abuse memories.[[65]](#cite_note-chu-65) When severe sexual abuse (penetration, several perpetrators, lasting more than one year) had occurred, dissociative symptoms were even more prominent.[[66]](#cite_note-draijer-66) Recent research showed that females with high exposure to child sexual abuse (CSA) develop PTSD symptoms that are associated with poor social functioning, which is also supported by prior research studies.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The feeling of being "cut-off" from peers and "emotional numbness" are both results of CSA and highly inhibit proper social functioning. Furthermore, PTSD is associated with higher risk of substance abuse as a result of the "self-medication hypothesis" and the "high-risk and susceptibility hypothesis".[[68]](#cite_note-auto-68)

Besides [dissociative identity disorder](/wiki/Dissociative_identity_disorder "Dissociative identity disorder") (DID), [post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder") (PTSD), and [complex post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Complex_post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Complex post-traumatic stress disorder") (C-PTSD), child sexual abuse survivors may present [borderline personality disorder](/wiki/Borderline_personality_disorder "Borderline personality disorder") (BPD) and eating disorders such as [bulimia nervosa](/wiki/Bulimia_nervosa "Bulimia nervosa").[[69]](#cite_note-Honor2010-69)

#### Research factors

Because child sexual abuse often occurs alongside other possibly confounding variables, such as poor family environment and physical abuse,[[70]](#cite_note-70)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*] some scholars argue it is important to control for those variables in studies which measure the effects of sexual abuse.[[24]](#cite_note-twin-24)[[53]](#cite_note-pmid11015813-53)[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72) In a 1998 review of related literature, Martin and Fleming state "The hypothesis advanced in this paper is that, in most cases, the fundamental damage inflicted by child sexual abuse is due to the child's developing capacities for trust, intimacy, agency and sexuality, and that many of the mental health problems of adult life associated with histories of child sexual abuse are second-order effects."[[73]](#cite_note-73) Other studies have found an independent association of child sexual abuse with adverse psychological outcomes.[[8]](#cite_note-levitan-8)[[24]](#cite_note-twin-24)[[53]](#cite_note-pmid11015813-53)

Kendler et al. (2000) found that most of the relationship between severe forms of child sexual abuse and adult psychopathology in their sample could not be explained by family discord, because the [effect size](/wiki/Effect_size "Effect size") of this association decreased only slightly after they controlled for possible confounding variables. Their examination of a small sample of CSA-discordant twins also supported a causal link between child sexual abuse and adult psychopathology; the CSA-exposed subjects had a consistently higher risk for psychopathologic disorders than their CSA non-exposed twins.[[53]](#cite_note-pmid11015813-53)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*]

A 1998 [meta-analysis by Bruce Rind et al.](/wiki/Rind_et_al._controversy "Rind et al. controversy") generated controversy by suggesting that child sexual abuse does not always cause pervasive harm, that girls were more likely to be psychologically harmed than boys, that some college students reported such encounters as positive experiences and that the extent of psychological damage depends on whether or not the child described the encounter as "consensual".[[74]](#cite_note-74) The study was criticized for flawed methodology and conclusions.[[75]](#cite_note-LC_Dallam-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) The US Congress condemned the study for its conclusions and for providing material used by pedophile organizations to justify their activities.[[77]](#cite_note-congress-77)

### Physical

#### Injury

Depending on the age and size of the child, and the degree of force used, child sexual abuse may cause internal lacerations and [bleeding](/wiki/Bleeding "Bleeding"). In severe cases, damage to internal organs may occur, which, in some cases, may cause death.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

#### Infections

Child sexual abuse may cause [sexually transmitted infections](/wiki/Sexually_transmitted_infection "Sexually transmitted infection").[[79]](#cite_note-jong-79) Due to a lack of sufficient [vaginal fluid](/wiki/Vaginal_lubrication "Vaginal lubrication"), chances of infections can heighten depending on the age and size of the child. [Vaginitis](/wiki/Vaginitis "Vaginitis") has also been reported.[[79]](#cite_note-jong-79)

#### Neurological damage

Research has shown that traumatic stress, including stress caused by sexual abuse, may cause notable changes in brain functioning and development.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81) Various studies have suggested that severe child sexual abuse may have a deleterious effect on brain development. Ito et al. (1998) found "reversed hemispheric asymmetry and greater left hemisphere coherence in abused subjects;"[[82]](#cite_note-82) Teicher et al. (1993) found that an increased likelihood of "ictal temporal lobe epilepsy-like symptoms" in abused subjects;[[83]](#cite_note-epi-83) Anderson et al. (2002) recorded abnormal [transverse relaxation](/wiki/Relaxation_(NMR) "Relaxation (NMR)") time in the cerebellar vermis of adults sexually abused in childhood;[[84]](#cite_note-84) Teicher et al. (1993) found that child sexual abuse was associated with a reduced [corpus callosum](/wiki/Corpus_callosum "Corpus callosum") area; various studies have found an association of reduced volume of the left hippocampus with child sexual abuse;[[85]](#cite_note-scars-85) and Ito et al. (1993) found increased electrophysiological abnormalities in sexually abused children.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Some studies indicate that sexual or physical abuse in children can lead to the overexcitation of an undeveloped [limbic system](/wiki/Limbic_system "Limbic system").[[85]](#cite_note-scars-85) Teicher et al. (1993)[[83]](#cite_note-epi-83) used the "Limbic System Checklist-33" to measure ictal temporal lobe epilepsy-like symptoms in 253 adults. Reports of child sexual abuse were associated with a 49% increase to LSCL-33 scores, 11% higher than the associated increase of self-reported physical abuse. Reports of both physical and sexual abuse were associated with a 113% increase. Male and female victims were similarly affected.[[83]](#cite_note-epi-83)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

Navalta et al. (2006) found that the self-reported math [Scholastic Aptitude Test](/wiki/Scholastic_Aptitude_Test "Scholastic Aptitude Test") scores of their sample of women with a history of repeated child sexual abuse were significantly lower than the self-reported math SAT scores of their non-abused sample. Because the abused subjects' verbal SAT scores were high, they hypothesized that the low math SAT scores could "stem from a defect in hemispheric integration." They also found a strong association between short-term memory impairments for all categories tested (verbal, visual, and global) and the duration of the abuse.[[88]](#cite_note-navalta-88)

## Incest

Main article: [Incest](/wiki/Incest "Incest")

Incest between a child or adolescent and a related adult is known as **child incestuous abuse**,[[89]](#cite_note-89) and has been identified as the most widespread form of child sexual abuse with a highly significant capacity to damage the young person.[[11]](#cite_note-Courtois-11) One researcher stated that more than 70% of abusers are immediate family members or someone very close to the family.[[90]](#cite_note-90) Another researcher stated that about 30% of all perpetrators of sexual abuse are related to their victim, 60% of the perpetrators are family acquaintances, like a neighbor, babysitter or friend and 10% of the perpetrators in child sexual abuse cases are strangers.[[14]](#cite_note-Whealin-14) A child sexual abuse offense where the perpetrator is related to the child, either by blood or marriage, is a form of [incest](/wiki/Incest "Incest") described as *intrafamilial child sexual abuse*.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

The most-often reported form of incest is father–daughter and stepfather–stepdaughter incest, with most of the remaining reports consisting of mother/stepmother–daughter/son incest.[[92]](#cite_note-Turner-92) Father–son incest is reported less often; however, it is not known if the actual prevalence is less or it is under-reported by a greater margin.[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) Similarly, some argue that sibling incest may be as common, or more common, than other types of incest: Goldman and Goldman[[97]](#cite_note-97) reported that 57% of incest involved siblings; Finkelhor reported that over 90% of [nuclear family](/wiki/Nuclear_family "Nuclear family") incest involved siblings;[[98]](#cite_note-98) while Cawson et al. show that sibling incest was reported twice as often as incest perpetrated by fathers/stepfathers.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

Prevalence of parental child sexual abuse is difficult to assess due to secrecy and privacy; some estimates state that 20 million Americans have been victimized by parental incest as children.[[92]](#cite_note-Turner-92)

## Types

Child sexual abuse involves a variety of sexual offenses, such as:

* *[sexual assault](/wiki/Sexual_assault "Sexual assault")* – a term defining offenses in which an adult uses a minor for the purpose of sexual gratification; for example, [rape](/wiki/Rape "Rape") (including [sodomy](/wiki/Sodomy "Sodomy")), and sexual penetration with an object.[[100]](#cite_note-100) US federal law and most US state laws define sexual assault of a minor as any form of penetration of the minor's [anus](/wiki/Anus "Anus") or [vagina](/wiki/Vagina "Vagina") or oral contact with the minor's [genitalia](/wiki/Sex_organ "Sex organ") for sexual purposes (excluding medical procedures such as [enemas](/wiki/Enema "Enema"), insertion of [suppositories](/wiki/Suppository "Suppository"), [rectal exams](/wiki/Rectal_examination "Rectal examination"), and [gynecological exams](/wiki/Gynaecology "Gynaecology")).[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102) This modern definition contrasts with prior gendered definitions defining rape and sexual assault exclusively as forceful penetration of a minor with a [penis](/wiki/Human_penis "Human penis").
* *[sexual exploitation](/wiki/Commercial_sexual_exploitation_of_children "Commercial sexual exploitation of children")* – a term defining offenses in which an adult victimizes a minor for advancement, sexual gratification, or profit; for example, prostituting a child,[[103]](#cite_note-103) [live streaming sexual abuse](/wiki/Live_streaming_sexual_abuse "Live streaming sexual abuse"),[[104]](#cite_note-104) and creating or trafficking in child pornography.[[105]](#cite_note-105)
* *[sextortion](/wiki/Sextortion "Sextortion")* – a term defining where children are threatened or blackmailed, most often with the possibility of sharing with the public a nude or sexual images of them, by a person who demands additional sexual content, sexual activity or money from the child.

### Commercial sexual exploitation

Main article: [Commercial sexual exploitation of children](/wiki/Commercial_sexual_exploitation_of_children "Commercial sexual exploitation of children")

Commercial sexual exploitation of children (CSEC) is defined by the Declaration of the First World Congress against Commercial Sexual Exploitation of Children, held in Stockholm in 1996, as "sexual abuse by an adult accompanied by remuneration in cash or in kind to the child or third person(s)."[[106]](#cite_note-106) CSEC usually takes the form of [child prostitution](/wiki/Child_prostitution "Child prostitution") or [child pornography](/wiki/Child_pornography "Child pornography"), and is often facilitated by [child sex tourism](/wiki/Child_sex_tourism "Child sex tourism"). CSEC is particularly a problem in developing countries of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia").[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108) In recent years, new innovations in technology have facilitated the trade of Internet child pornography.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

In the United Kingdom, the term *child sexual exploitation* covers any form of sexual abuse which includes an exchange of a resource for sexual activity with a child.[[110]](#cite_note-Williams_discussion-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111) Prior to 2009, the term commonly used to describe child sexual exploitation was *child prostitution*.[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113) The term *child sexual exploitation* first appeared in government guidance in 2009 as part of an attempt to promote an understanding that children involved in exploitation were victims of abuse rather than criminals.[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-Words_Matter-115) Because early definitions of child sexual exploitation were created to foster a move away from use of the term *child prostitution*, the concept of exchange, which made child sexual exploitation different from child sexual abuse, referred to financial gain only. However, in the years since the birth of the concept of child sexual exploitation, the notion of exchange has been widened to include other types of gain, including love, acquisition of status and protection from harm.[[115]](#cite_note-Words_Matter-115)

## Disclosure

Children who received supportive responses following disclosure had less traumatic symptoms and were abused for a shorter period of time than children who did not receive support.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117) In general, studies have found that children need support and stress-reducing resources after disclosure of sexual abuse.[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-pwmjhq-119) Negative social reactions to disclosure have been found to be harmful to the survivor's well-being.[[120]](#cite_note-120) One study reported that children who received a bad reaction from the first person they told, especially if the person was a close family member, had worse scores as adults on general trauma symptoms, post traumatic stress disorder symptoms, and dissociation.[[121]](#cite_note-121) Another study found that in most cases when children did disclose abuse, the person they talked to did not respond effectively, blamed or rejected the child, and took little or no action to stop the abuse.[[119]](#cite_note-pwmjhq-119) Non-validating and otherwise non-supportive responses to disclosure by the child's primary attachment figure may indicate a relational disturbance predating the sexual abuse that may have been a risk factor for the abuse, and which can remain a risk factor for its psychological consequences.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

The [American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Child_and_Adolescent_Psychiatry "American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry") provides guidelines for what to say to the victim and what to do following the disclosure.[[123]](#cite_note-123) As Don Brown has indicated: "A minimization of the trauma and its effects is commonly injected into the picture by parental caregivers to shelter and calm the child. It has been commonly assumed that focusing on children's issues too long will negatively impact their recovery. Therefore, the parental caregiver teaches the child to mask his or her issues."[[124]](#cite_note-124)

In many jurisdictions, abuse that is suspected, not necessarily proven, requires reporting to [child protection](/wiki/Child_protection "Child protection") agencies, such as the [Child Protection Services](/wiki/Child_Protection_Services "Child Protection Services") in the United States. Recommendations for healthcare workers, such as [primary care providers](/wiki/Primary_care_provider "Primary care provider") and [nurses](/wiki/Nurse "Nurse"), who are often suited to encounter suspected abuse are advised to firstly determine the child's immediate need for safety. A private environment away from suspected abusers is desired for interviewing and examining. Leading statements that can distort the story are avoided. As disclosing abuse can be distressing and sometimes even shameful, reassuring the child that he or she has done the right thing by telling and that they are not bad and that the abuse was not their fault helps in disclosing more information. Anatomically correct dolls are sometimes used to help explain what happened. However, some researchers have found that the use of these dolls may be too graphic and overstimulating, which may lead children that were not abused to behave as though they were sexually abused.[[125]](#cite_note-Segal-125) For the suspected abusers, it is also recommended to use a nonjudgmental, nonthreatening attitude towards them and to withhold expressing shock, in order to help disclose information.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

## Treatment

Main article: [Post-assault treatment of sexual assault victims](/wiki/Post-assault_treatment_of_sexual_assault_victims "Post-assault treatment of sexual assault victims")

The initial approach to treating a person who has been a victim of sexual abuse is dependent upon several important factors:

* Age at the time of presentation
* Circumstances of presentation for treatment
* Co-morbid conditions

The goal of treatment is not only to treat current mental health issues, and [trauma](/wiki/Psychological_trauma "Psychological trauma") related symptoms, but also to prevent future ones.

### Children and adolescents

Children often present for treatment in one of several circumstances, including criminal investigations, custody battles, problematic behaviors, and referrals from child welfare agencies.[[127]](#cite_note-NCCAN-127)

The three major modalities for therapy with children and adolescents are [family therapy](/wiki/Family_therapy "Family therapy"), [group therapy](/wiki/Group_psychotherapy "Group psychotherapy"), and [individual therapy](/wiki/Psychotherapy "Psychotherapy"). Which course is used depends on a variety of factors that must be assessed on a case-by-case basis. For instance, treatment of young children generally requires strong parental involvement and can benefit from family therapy. Adolescents tend to be more independent; they can benefit from individual or group therapy. The modality also shifts during the course of treatment; for example, group therapy is rarely used in the initial stages, as the subject matter is very personal and/or embarrassing.[[127]](#cite_note-NCCAN-127) In a 2012 systematic review, [cognitive behavior therapy](/wiki/Cognitive_behavioral_therapy "Cognitive behavioral therapy") showed potential in treating the adverse consequences of child sexual abuse.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

Major factors that affect both the pathology and response to treatment include the type and severity of the sexual act, its frequency, the age at which it occurred, and the child's family of origin. Roland C. Summit, a medical doctor, defined the different stages the victims of child sexual abuse go through, called [child sexual abuse accommodation syndrome](/wiki/Child_sexual_abuse_accommodation_syndrome "Child sexual abuse accommodation syndrome"). He suggested that children who are victims of sexual abuse display a range of symptoms that include secrecy, helplessness, entrapment, accommodation, delayed and conflicted disclosure and recantation.[[129]](#cite_note-Summit-129)

### Adults

Adults who have been sexually abused as children often present for treatment with a secondary mental health issue, which can include [substance abuse](/wiki/Substance_abuse "Substance abuse"), [eating disorders](/wiki/Eating_disorders "Eating disorders"), [personality disorders](/wiki/Personality_disorders "Personality disorders"), [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"), and conflict in romantic or interpersonal relationships.[[130]](#cite_note-Swaby--130)

Generally, the approach is to focus on the present problem, rather than the abuse itself. Treatment is highly varied and depends on the person's specific issues. For instance, a person with a history of sexual abuse and severe depression would be treated for [depression](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder"). However, there is often an emphasis on [cognitive restructuring](/wiki/Cognitive_restructuring "Cognitive restructuring") due to the deep-seated nature of the trauma. Some newer techniques such as [eye movement desensitization and reprocessing](/wiki/Eye_movement_desensitization_and_reprocessing "Eye movement desensitization and reprocessing") (EMDR) have been shown to be effective.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

Although there is no known cure for pedophilia,[[132]](#cite_note-SetoReview-132) there are [a number of treatments for pedophiles and child sexual abusers](/wiki/Pedophilia#Treatment "Pedophilia"). Some of the treatments focus on attempting to change the sexual preference of pedophiles, while others focus on keeping pedophiles from committing child sexual abuse, or on keeping child sexual abusers from committing child sexual abuse again. [Cognitive behavioral therapy](/wiki/Cognitive_behavioral_therapy "Cognitive behavioral therapy") (CBT), for example, aims to reduce attitudes, beliefs, and behaviors that may increase the likelihood of sexual offenses against children. Its content varies widely between therapists, but a typical program might involve training in self-control, social competence and empathy, and use [cognitive restructuring](/wiki/Cognitive_restructuring "Cognitive restructuring") to change views on sex with children. The most common form of this therapy is [relapse prevention](/wiki/Relapse_prevention "Relapse prevention"), where the patient is taught to identify and respond to potentially risky situations based on principles used for treating addictions.[[133]](#cite_note-seto171-133)

The evidence for cognitive behavioral therapy is mixed.[[133]](#cite_note-seto171-133) A 2012 [Cochrane Review](/wiki/Cochrane_Collaboration "Cochrane Collaboration") of randomized trials found that CBT had no effect on risk of reoffending for contact sex offenders.[[134]](#cite_note-cochrane-134) Meta-analyses in 2002 and 2005, which included both randomized and non-randomized studies, concluded that CBT reduced recidivism.[[135]](#cite_note-135)[[136]](#cite_note-136) There is debate over whether non-randomized studies should be considered informative.[[132]](#cite_note-SetoReview-132)[[137]](#cite_note-rice2012-137) More research is needed.[[134]](#cite_note-cochrane-134)

## Prevention

Main article: [Initiatives to prevent sexual violence](/wiki/Initiatives_to_prevent_sexual_violence "Initiatives to prevent sexual violence")

Child sexual abuse prevention programmes were developed in the United States of America during the 1970s. Some programme are delivered to children and can include one-to-one work[[138]](#cite_note-Williams_one-138) and group work.[[139]](#cite_note-Williams_group-139) Programmes delivered to parents were developed in the 1980s and took the form of one-off meetings, two to three hours long.[[140]](#cite_note-Babatsikos-140)[[141]](#cite_note-Hébert-141)[[142]](#cite_note-Wurtele_Moreno_Kenny-142)[[143]](#cite_note-Wurtele_Kenny_2010_pp._130–152-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145) In the last 15 years, web-based programmes have been developed. School-based education programs were evaluated in 2015 by Cochrane that demonstrated improvements in protective behaviors and knowledge among children.[[146]](#cite_note-146) The American CDC lists that improving surveillance systems can help monitor and prevent child abuse.[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148) While progress has been made in raising awareness and implementing preventive measures, challenges persist in identifying and prosecuting perpetrators, supporting victims, and addressing systemic factors contributing to abuse. Cultural and societal stigmas, coupled with underreporting and insufficient resources, further complicate the landscape. Additionally, the rapid evolution of technology introduces new challenges, such as online exploitation and grooming. The National Center for Missing and Exploited Children (NCMEC) combats child sexual abuse and exploitation through a range of initiatives including providing assistance to law enforcement, offering resources and support to families of missing and exploited children, raising public awareness, facilitating prevention programs, and operating a hotline for reporting and responding to incidents of child sexual exploitation.[[149]](#cite_note-149) Despite advancements in understanding and addressing CSA, a more comprehensive and coordinated approach is needed to effectively combat this deeply concerning issue and ensure the safety and well-being of all children. Legislative efforts like the Child Abuse Prevention and Treatment Act (CAPTA), originally enacted in 1974 and subsequently amended, provide federal funding and guidance to states for prevention, investigation, and treatment activities.[[150]](#cite_note-150) Erin's Law, enacted in 38 states, mandates prevention-oriented CSA programs in public schools, illustrating ongoing efforts to address this critical issue at both federal and state levels.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

## Offenders

"Child sex offender" redirects here. For sex crimes committed by minors, see [Juvenile sex crimes](/wiki/Juvenile_sex_crimes "Juvenile sex crimes").

### Demographics

Offenders are more likely to be relatives or acquaintances of their victim than strangers.[[152]](#cite_note-152) A 2006–07 Idaho study of 430 cases found that 82% of juvenile sex offenders were known to the victims (acquaintances 46% or relatives 36%).[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154) In the Netherlands only 7% of offenders were strangers to the victims.[[155]](#cite_note-ontuchtrechter-155)

More offenders are male than female, though the percentage varies between studies. In the Netherlands 3% of the [convicted](/wiki/Conviction "Conviction") perpetrators were women.[[155]](#cite_note-ontuchtrechter-155) A 2004 synthesis of studies of sexual misconduct in US schools showed estimates between 4% and 43% of offenders being female.[[156]](#cite_note-Shakeshaft25-156) [Gender roles](/wiki/Gender_role "Gender role"), gendered [under-reporting](/wiki/Under-reporting#Crime "Under-reporting") and [sentencing disparity](/wiki/Sentencing_disparity "Sentencing disparity") cause an underestimate in the share of female perpetrators according to a 2003 study.[[157]](#cite_note-c065-157) A 2022 review found female perpetrators as under-reported and under-researched.[[158]](#cite_note-u536-158)

In the Netherlands 14.58% of the victims were boys.[[155]](#cite_note-ontuchtrechter-155) According to research conducted in Australia by Kelly Richards on child sexual abuse, 35.1% of female victims were abused by another male relative and 16.4% of male victims were abused by another male relative.[[159]](#cite_note-159)

In Netherlands one in six perpetrators was underage.[[160]](#cite_note-160) In U.S. schools, educators who offend range in age from "21 to 75 years old, with an average age of 28".[[161]](#cite_note-161)

### Typology

Early research in the 1970s and 1980s began to classify offenders based on their motivations and traits. Groth and Birnbaum (1978) categorized child sexual offenders into two groups, "fixated" and "regressed".[[162]](#cite_note-Groth-162) Fixated were described as having a primary attraction to children, whereas regressed had largely maintained relationships with other adults, and were even married. This study also showed that adult [sexual orientation](/wiki/Sexual_orientation "Sexual orientation") was not related to the sex of the victim targeted, e.g. men who molested boys often had adult relationships with women.[[162]](#cite_note-Groth-162)

Later work (Holmes and Holmes, 2002) expanded on the types of offenders and their psychological profiles. They are divided as follows:[[163]](#cite_note-holmes-163)

* Situational – does not prefer children, but offend under certain conditions.
  + *Regressed* – Typically has relationships with adults, but a stressor causes them to seek children as a substitute.
  + *Morally indiscriminate* – All-around sexual deviant, who may commit other sexual offenses unrelated to children.
  + *Naive/Inadequate* – Often mentally disabled in some way, finds children less threatening.
* Preferential – has true sexual interest in children.
  + *Mysoped* – Sadistic and violent, target strangers more often than acquaintances.
  + *Fixated* – Little or no activity with own age, described as an "overgrown child".

### Causal factors

Causal factors of child sex offenders are not known conclusively.[[164]](#cite_note-psychtoday2008-164) The experience of sexual abuse as a child was previously thought to be a strong risk factor, but research does not show a causal relationship, as the vast majority of sexually abused children do not grow up to be adult offenders, nor do the majority of adult offenders report childhood sexual abuse. The US [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office "Government Accountability Office") concluded, "the existence of a cycle of sexual abuse was not established." Before 1996, there was greater belief in the theory of a "cycle of violence", because most of the research done was retrospective—abusers were asked if they had experienced past abuse. Even the majority of studies found that most adult sex offenders said they had *not* been sexually abused during childhood, but studies varied in terms of their estimates of the percentage of such offenders who had been abused, from 0 to 79 percent. More recent prospective [longitudinal](/wiki/Longitudinal_study "Longitudinal study") research—studying children with documented cases of sexual abuse over time to determine what percentage become adult offenders—has demonstrated that the cycle of violence theory is not an adequate explanation for why people molest children.[[165]](#cite_note-165)

Offenders may use [cognitive distortions](/wiki/Cognitive_distortion "Cognitive distortion") to facilitate their offenses, such as [minimization](/wiki/Minimisation_(psychology) "Minimisation (psychology)") of the abuse, [victim blaming](/wiki/Victim_blaming "Victim blaming"), and [excuses](/wiki/Making_excuses "Making excuses").[[166]](#cite_note-166)

### Treatment

#### Cognitive behavioral therapy

[Cognitive behavioral therapy](/wiki/Cognitive_behavioral_therapy "Cognitive behavioral therapy") (CBT) aims to reduce attitudes, beliefs, and behaviors that may increase the likelihood of sexual offenses against children. Its content varies widely between therapists, but a typical program might involve training in self-control, social competence and empathy, and use [cognitive restructuring](/wiki/Cognitive_restructuring "Cognitive restructuring") to change views on sex with children. The most common form of this therapy is [relapse prevention](/wiki/Relapse_prevention "Relapse prevention"), where the patient is taught to identify and respond to potentially risky situations based on principles used for treating addictions.[[167]](#cite_note-seto-167): 171

The evidence for cognitive behavioral therapy is mixed.[[167]](#cite_note-seto-167): 171 A 2012 [Cochrane Review](/wiki/Cochrane_Collaboration "Cochrane Collaboration") of randomized trials found that CBT had no effect on risk of reoffending for contact sex offenders.[[168]](#cite_note-cochrane2-168) Meta-analyses in 2002 and 2005, which included both randomized and non-randomized studies, concluded that CBT reduced recidivism.[[169]](#cite_note-169)[[170]](#cite_note-170) There is debate over whether non-randomized studies should be considered informative.[[171]](#cite_note-SetoReview2-171)[[172]](#cite_note-rice20122-172) More research is needed.[[168]](#cite_note-cochrane2-168)

#### Behavioral interventions

Behavioral treatments target sexual arousal to children, using satiation and aversion techniques to suppress sexual arousal to children and [covert sensitization](/wiki/Covert_sensitization "Covert sensitization") (or [masturbatory](/wiki/Masturbation "Masturbation") reconditioning) to increase sexual arousal to adults.[[167]](#cite_note-seto-167): 175 Behavioral treatments appear to have an effect on sexual arousal patterns during phallometric testing, but it is not known whether the effect represents changes in sexual interests or changes in the ability to control genital arousal during testing, nor whether the effect persists in the long term.[[173]](#cite_note-173)[[174]](#cite_note-174) For sex offenders with mental disabilities, [applied behavior analysis](/wiki/Applied_behavior_analysis "Applied behavior analysis") has been used.[[175]](#cite_note-175)

#### Sex drive reduction

See also: [Chemical castration](/wiki/Chemical_castration "Chemical castration")

Pharmacological interventions are used to lower the sex drive in general, which can ease the management of pedophilic feelings, but does not change sexual preference.[[176]](#cite_note-camilleri199-176) [Antiandrogens](/wiki/Antiandrogens "Antiandrogens") work by interfering with the activity of testosterone. [Cyproterone acetate](/wiki/Cyproterone_acetate "Cyproterone acetate") (Androcur) and [medroxyprogesterone acetate](/wiki/Medroxyprogesterone_acetate "Medroxyprogesterone acetate") (Depo-Provera) are the most commonly used. The efficacy of antiandrogens has some support, but few high-quality studies exist. Cyproterone acetate has the strongest evidence for reducing sexual arousal, while findings on medroxyprogesterone acetate have been mixed.[[167]](#cite_note-seto-167): 177–181

[Gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogs](/wiki/Gonadotropin-releasing_hormone_analog "Gonadotropin-releasing hormone analog") such as [leuprorelin](/wiki/Leuprorelin "Leuprorelin") (Lupron), which last longer and have fewer side-effects, are also used to reduce [libido](/wiki/Libido "Libido"),[[177]](#cite_note-pmid15985890-177) as are [selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors](/wiki/Selective_serotonin_reuptake_inhibitor "Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor").[[167]](#cite_note-seto-167): 177–181 The evidence for these alternatives is more limited and mostly based on open trials and case studies.[[171]](#cite_note-SetoReview2-171) All of these treatments, commonly referred to as "[chemical castration](/wiki/Chemical_castration "Chemical castration")", are often used in conjunction with cognitive behavioral therapy.[[178]](#cite_note-178) According to the [Association for the Treatment of Sexual Abusers](/wiki/Association_for_the_Treatment_of_Sexual_Abusers "Association for the Treatment of Sexual Abusers"), when treating child molesters, "anti-androgen treatment should be coupled with appropriate monitoring and counseling within a comprehensive treatment plan."[[179]](#cite_note-atsacast-179) These drugs may have side-effects, such as weight gain, breast development, liver damage and osteoporosis.[[171]](#cite_note-SetoReview2-171)

Historically, surgical [castration](/wiki/Castration "Castration") was used to lower sex drive by reducing testosterone. The emergence of pharmacological methods of adjusting testosterone has made it largely obsolete, because they are similarly effective and less invasive.[[176]](#cite_note-camilleri199-176) It is still occasionally performed in Germany, the Czech Republic, Switzerland, and a few U.S. states. Non-randomized studies have reported that surgical castration reduces recidivism in contact sex offenders.[[167]](#cite_note-seto-167): 181–182, 192 The Association for the Treatment of Sexual Abusers opposes surgical castration[[179]](#cite_note-atsacast-179) and the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") works to bring the practice to an end in Eastern European countries where it is still applied through the courts.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

### Pedophilia

Main article: [Pedophilia](/wiki/Pedophilia "Pedophilia")

Pedophilia is a condition in which an adult or older adolescent is primarily or exclusively attracted to prepubescent children, whether the attraction is acted upon or not.[[181]](#cite_note-181)[[182]](#cite_note-182) A person with this [paraphilia](/wiki/Paraphilia "Paraphilia") is called a *pedophile*.

In [law enforcement](/wiki/Police "Police"), the term *pedophile* is sometimes used to describe those accused or convicted of child sexual abuse under [sociolegal definitions of *child*](/wiki/Minor_(law) "Minor (law)") (including both prepubescent children and adolescents younger than the local [age of consent](/wiki/Age_of_consent "Age of consent"));[[17]](#cite_note-ames-17) however, not all child sexual offenders are pedophiles and not all pedophiles engage in sexual abuse of children.[[18]](#cite_note-barbaree-seto-18)[[183]](#cite_note-apaDSM-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184) For these reasons, researchers recommend against imprecisely describing all child molesters as *pedophiles*.[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186)

The term *pedocriminality* ([De](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Pädokriminalität*; [Fr](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *pédocriminalité*) is a controversial term which originated in the 1980s and has been used by organisations such as [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF"), [UNHRC](/wiki/UNHRC "UNHRC"), the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization")[[187]](#cite_note-187) and the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe")[[188]](#cite_note-188) to refer to child sexual abuse and sexual violence used against children,[[189]](#cite_note-189)[[190]](#cite_note-Gallwitz-190) [child prostitution](/wiki/Child_prostitution "Child prostitution"), [child trafficking](/wiki/Child_trafficking "Child trafficking") and the use of [child pornography](/wiki/Child_pornography "Child pornography").[[191]](#cite_note-191) The term "cyber-pedocriminality" has been used to refer to the activities of viewers of child pornography online.[[192]](#cite_note-192)

### Recidivism

Although reconviction data suggests that most sex offenders do not reoffend,[[193]](#cite_note-193) [OJP](/wiki/Office_of_Justice_Programs "Office of Justice Programs") reported that observed [recidivism](/wiki/Recidivism "Recidivism") rates of sex offenders are underestimated of actual reoffending.[[194]](#cite_note-bjs-raso-194) Estimated rates among child sex offenders vary by surveys and it is difficult to estimate accurately. One study found that 42% of offenders re-offended (either a sex crime, violent crime, or both) after they were released. Risk for re-offense was highest in the first 6 years after release, but continued to be significant even 10–31 years later, with 23% offending during this time.[[195]](#cite_note-195) A study done in California in 1965 found an 18.2% recidivism rate for offenders targeting the opposite sex and a 34.5% recidivism rate for same-sex offenders after 5 years.[[196]](#cite_note-196)

Because recidivism is defined and measured differently from study to study, one can arrive at inaccurate conclusions being made based on comparison of two or more studies that are not conducted with similar methodology.[[197]](#cite_note-197)

### Other children

Main article: [Child-on-child sexual abuse](/wiki/Child-on-child_sexual_abuse "Child-on-child sexual abuse")

When a prepubescent child is sexually abused by one or more other children or adolescent youths, and no adult is directly involved, it is defined as [child-on-child sexual abuse](/wiki/Child-on-child_sexual_abuse "Child-on-child sexual abuse"). The definition includes any sexual activity between children that occurs without [consent](/wiki/Consent "Consent"), without equality, or due to [coercion](/wiki/Coercion "Coercion"),[[198]](#cite_note-shaw-198) whether the offender uses physical force, threats, trickery or [emotional manipulation](/wiki/Psychological_manipulation "Psychological manipulation") to compel cooperation. When sexual abuse is perpetrated by one sibling upon another, it is known as "[intersibling abuse](/wiki/Intersibling_abuse "Intersibling abuse")", a form of [incest](/wiki/Incest "Incest").[[199]](#cite_note-Caffaroa-199)

Unlike research on adult offenders, a strong causal relationship has been established between child and adolescent offenders and these offenders' own prior victimization, by either adults or other children.[[200]](#cite_note-gray2-200)[[201]](#cite_note-gray-201)[[202]](#cite_note-Bromberg-202)[[203]](#cite_note-203)
