Main article: [Sexual abuse in primary and secondary schools](/wiki/Sexual_abuse_in_primary_and_secondary_schools "Sexual abuse in primary and secondary schools")

According to a 2010 [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF") report, 46% of Congolese schoolgirls confirmed that they had been victims of sexual harassment, abuse, and violence committed by their teachers or other school personnel.[[204]](#cite_note-fp-204) In [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique"), a study by the Ministry of Education found that 70 percent of female respondents reported knowing teachers who use sexual intercourse as a necessary condition to advance students to the next grade.[[204]](#cite_note-fp-204) A survey by Promundo found that 16% of girls in [North Kivu](/wiki/North_Kivu "North Kivu") said they had been forced to have sex with their teachers.[[204]](#cite_note-fp-204) According to UNICEF, teachers in [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali") are known to use "La menace du bic rouge" ("the threat of the red pen"), using the threat of bad grades to coerce girls into acquiescing to sexual advances.[[204]](#cite_note-fp-204) According to Plan International, 16% of children in [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo"), for instance, named a teacher as responsible for the pregnancy of a classmate.[[204]](#cite_note-fp-204)

## Prevalence

### Global

Based on [self-disclosure data](/wiki/Self-report_study "Self-report study"), a 2011 [meta-analysis](/wiki/Meta-analysis "Meta-analysis") of 217 studies between 1980 and 2008 estimated a global prevalence of 12.7%–18% for girls and 7.6% for boys. The rates of self-disclosed abuse for specific continents were as follows:[[205]](#cite_note-stolt-205)

| Region | Girls | Boys |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Africa | 20.2% | 19.3% |
| Asia | 11.3% | 4.1% |
| Australia | 21.5% | 7.5% |
| Europe | 13.5% | 5.6% |
| South America | 13.4% | 13.8% |
| US/Canada | 20.1% | 8% |

A 2009 meta-analysis of 65 studies from 22 countries found a global prevalence of 19.7% for females and 7.9% for males for some form of child sexual abuse prior to the age of 18. In that analysis, Africa had the highest prevalence rate of child sexual abuse (34.4%), primarily because of high rates in South Africa; Europe showed the lowest prevalence rate (9.2%); and America and Asia had prevalence rates between 10.1% and 23.9%.[[206]](#cite_note-pereda-206)

Some scientists argue that prevalence rates are much higher, and that many cases of child abuse are [never reported](/wiki/Dark_figure_of_crime "Dark figure of crime"). One study found that professionals failed to report approximately 40% of the child sexual abuse cases they encountered.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuehnle1996[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_November_2013]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (November_2013)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-207)

### Africa

Further information: [Virgin cleansing myth](/wiki/Virgin_cleansing_myth "Virgin cleansing myth")

See also: [Sexual violence in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Sexual_violence_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Sexual violence in the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), [Sexual violence in South Africa](/wiki/Sexual_violence_in_South_Africa "Sexual violence in South Africa"), and [Child marriage](/wiki/Child_marriage "Child marriage")

A ten-country school-based study in southern Africa in 2007 found 19.6% of female students and 21.1% of male students aged 11–16 years reported they had experienced forced or coerced sex. Rates among 16-year-olds were
28.8% in females and 25.4% in males. Comparing the same schools in eight countries between 2003 and 2007, age-standardised on the 2007 Botswana male sample, there was no significant decrease between 2003 and 2007 among females in any country and inconsistent changes among males.[[208]](#cite_note-208)

The prevalence of child sexual abuse in Africa is compounded by the [virgin cleansing myth](/wiki/Virgin_cleansing_myth "Virgin cleansing myth") that sexual intercourse with a virgin will cure a man of [HIV](/wiki/HIV "HIV") or [AIDS](/wiki/AIDS "AIDS"). The myth is prevalent in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe"),[[209]](#cite_note-209) [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia") and [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") and is being blamed for the high rate of sexual abuse against young children.[[210]](#cite_note-210)

In November 2007, [Thomson Reuters Foundation](/wiki/Thomson_Reuters_Foundation "Thomson Reuters Foundation") reported that [child rape is on the rise](/wiki/Sexual_violence_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Sexual violence in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") in the war-ravaged eastern [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo").[[211]](#cite_note-211) [Aid workers](/wiki/Humanitarian_aid "Humanitarian aid") blame combatants on all sides, who operate with much impunity, for a culture of [sexual violence](/wiki/Sexual_violence "Sexual violence").[[212]](#cite_note-212) [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") has some of the highest incidences of child rape (including the rape of babies) in the world (also see [sexual violence in South Africa](/wiki/Sexual_violence_in_South_Africa "Sexual violence in South Africa")).[[213]](#cite_note-time-213) A survey by CIET found around 11% of boys and 4% of girls admitted to forcing someone else to have sex with them.[[213]](#cite_note-time-213) In a related [survey](/wiki/Statistical_survey "Statistical survey") conducted among 1,500 schoolchildren, a quarter of all the boys interviewed said that "jackrolling", a term for [gang rape](/wiki/Gang_rape "Gang rape"), was fun.[[214]](#cite_note-bbc1-214) More than 67,000 cases of rape and sexual assaults against children were reported in 2000 in South Africa, compared to 37,500 in 1998. Child welfare groups believe that the number of unreported incidents could be up to 10 times that number. The largest increase in attacks was against children under seven. The [virgin cleansing myth](/wiki/Virgin_cleansing_myth "Virgin cleansing myth") is especially common in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), which has the highest number of HIV-positive citizens in the world. [Eastern Cape](/wiki/Eastern_Cape "Eastern Cape") social worker Edith Kriel notes that "child abusers are often relatives of their victims – even their fathers and providers."[[215]](#cite_note-Flanagan-215)

A number of high-profile baby rapes appeared since 2001 (including the fact that they required extensive reconstructive surgery to rebuild urinary, genital, abdominal, or tracheal systems). In 2001, a 9-month-old was raped and likely lost consciousness as the pain was too much to bear.[[216]](#cite_note-216) In February 2002, an 8-month-old infant was reportedly gang-raped by four men. One has been charged. The [infant](/wiki/Infant "Infant") has required extensive reconstructive surgery. The 8-month-old infant's injuries were so extensive, increased attention on prosecution has occurred.[[217]](#cite_note-medscape-217)

### Asia

Further information: [Child prostitution in Thailand](/wiki/Child_prostitution_in_Thailand "Child prostitution in Thailand"), [Child pornography laws in Japan](/wiki/Child_pornography_laws_in_Japan "Child pornography laws in Japan"), and [Ages of consent in Asia](/wiki/Ages_of_consent_in_Asia "Ages of consent in Asia")

In [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), some boys are forced to participate in [sexual activities with men](/wiki/Bacha_bazi "Bacha bazi"). They are also termed 'dancing boys'. The custom is connected to sexual slavery and child prostitution.[[218]](#cite_note-218)[[219]](#cite_note-Foreign_Policy-219)

In [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), child prostitutes are known to take the drug [Oradexon](/wiki/Dexamethasone "Dexamethasone"), an over-the-counter [steroid](/wiki/Steroid "Steroid"), usually used by farmers to fatten cattle, to [make child prostitutes look larger and older](/wiki/Prostitution_in_Bangladesh "Prostitution in Bangladesh"). Charities say that 90% of prostitutes in the country's legalized brothels use the drug. According to social activists, the steroid can cause [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes") and [high blood pressure](/wiki/High_blood_pressure "High blood pressure") and is highly addictive.[[220]](#cite_note-220)[[221]](#cite_note-221)[[222]](#cite_note-222)

In 2007, the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") [Ministry of Women and Child Development](/wiki/Ministry_of_Women_and_Child_Development_(India) "Ministry of Women and Child Development (India)") published the "Study on Child Abuse: India 2007".[[223]](#cite_note-IndiaStudy2007-223) It sampled 12447 children, 2324 young adults and 2449 stakeholders across 13 states. It looked at different forms of child abuse: [physical abuse](/wiki/Physical_abuse "Physical abuse"), [sexual abuse](/wiki/Sexual_abuse "Sexual abuse") and [emotional abuse](/wiki/Psychological_abuse "Psychological abuse") and girl [child neglect](/wiki/Child_neglect "Child neglect") in five evidence groups, namely, children in a family environment, children in school, children at work, children on the street and children in institutions. The study's[[223]](#cite_note-IndiaStudy2007-223) main findings included: 53.22% of children reported having faced sexual abuse. Among them, 52.94% were boys and 47.06% girls. [Andhra Pradesh](/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh"), [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam"), [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") and [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi") reported the highest percentage of sexual abuse among both boys and girls, as well as the highest incidence of sexual assaults. 21.90% of child respondents faced severe forms of sexual abuse, 5.69% had been sexually assaulted and 50.76% reported other forms of sexual abuse. Children on the street, at work and in institutional care reported the highest incidence of sexual assault. The study also reported that 50% of abusers are known to the child or are in a position of trust and responsibility and most children had not reported the matter to anyone. Despite years of lack of any specific [child sexual abuse laws in India](/wiki/Child_sexual_abuse_laws_in_India "Child sexual abuse laws in India"), which treated them separately from adults in case of sexual offense, the [Protection of Children Against Sexual Offences Bill, 2011](/wiki/Protection_of_Children_from_Sexual_Offenses_Act "Protection of Children from Sexual Offenses Act") was passed the [Indian parliament](/wiki/Indian_parliament "Indian parliament") on May 22, 2012, which came into force from 14 November 2012.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

According to research published in 2019 India had the largest number of child sexual abuse imagery searches along with being responsible for producing a third of the worlds child sexual abuse content online.[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-226)[[227]](#cite_note-227)

In [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), sexual abuse of children is a problem in some [madrassas](/wiki/Madrassas_in_Pakistan "Madrassas in Pakistan").[[228]](#cite_note-sex_abuse-228)[[229]](#cite_note-229) Child sexual abuse has also been reported in Madrassas across Bangladesh and India.[[230]](#cite_note-prothomalo-230)[[231]](#cite_note-Siasat-231)[[232]](#cite_note-FE-232)[[233]](#cite_note-IT-233)

The [Kasur child sexual abuse scandal](/wiki/Kasur_child_sexual_abuse_scandal "Kasur child sexual abuse scandal"), which involved [forced sex acts](/wiki/Forced_sex "Forced sex") and an estimated 280 to 300 children, was termed the largest child abuse scandal in Pakistan's history.[[234]](#cite_note-234)

In 2019, Pakistan's Human Rights Minister, Shirin Mazari has said that Pakistan was ranked as the country with the largest numbers of [child pornography](/wiki/Child_pornography "Child pornography") viewers.[[235]](#cite_note-HRM-235) Geo Pakistan, the [Federal Investigation Agency](/wiki/Federal_Investigation_Agency "Federal Investigation Agency"), cyber-crime chief has said, "Child pornography is a business . . with those involved in the crime linked to international child pornography rings."[[236]](#cite_note-FIA-236)

In [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), a survey of adolescents reported 2.5% as having experienced childhood sexual abuse.[[237]](#cite_note-237)

In China, although the prevalence of CSA is not low, ranging from 9% to 18%, most cases are not disclosed or reported in official national statistics. According to reports published by the [NGO](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization") Protection of Girls, only 2,952 cases of CSA were publicly reported in the media from 2013 to 2021, involving approximately 5,500 child victims, while [Peking University](/wiki/Peking_University "Peking University")'s "PKU Law" database recorded over 17,000 cases from 1991 to 2018.[[238]](#cite_note-238)

In [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"), the UK Ambassador [Craig Murray](/wiki/Craig_Murray "Craig Murray") wrote that the government, under president [Islam Karimov](/wiki/Islam_Karimov "Islam Karimov"), used child rape to force false confessions from prisoners.[[239]](#cite_note-239)

### Pacific

See also: [Sexual violence in Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Sexual_violence_in_Papua_New_Guinea "Sexual violence in Papua New Guinea")

According to UNICEF, nearly half of [reported rape victims](/wiki/Sexual_violence_in_Papua_New_Guinea "Sexual violence in Papua New Guinea") in [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea") are under 15 years of age and 13% are under 7 years of age[[240]](#cite_note-UNICEF2-240) while a report by [ChildFund](/wiki/ChildFund "ChildFund") Australia citing former Parliamentarian Dame [Carol Kidu](/wiki/Carol_Kidu "Carol Kidu") stated 50% of those seeking medical help after rape are under 16, 25% are under 10 and 10% are under 8.[[241]](#cite_note-241)

Additionally, a study found that men with a history of victimization, especially having been raped or otherwise sexually coerced themselves, were more likely than otherwise to have participated in both single-perpetrator and [multiple-perpetrator](/wiki/Gang_rape "Gang rape") non-partner rape.[[242]](#cite_note-Jewkes-242) 57·5% (587/1022) of men who raped a non-partner committed their first rape as teenagers.[[242]](#cite_note-Jewkes-242)

### United States

Child sexual abuse occurs frequently in Western society,[[243]](#cite_note-243) although the rate of prevalence can be difficult to determine.[[244]](#cite_note-Finklehor-epid-244)[[245]](#cite_note-245)[[246]](#cite_note-Kevin_M._Gorey_and_Donald_R._Leslie_1997_pp391–398-246)

The estimates for the United States vary widely.

Research in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") has concluded that approximately 15% to 25% of women and 5% to 15% of men were sexually abused when they were children.[[14]](#cite_note-Whealin-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Finkelhor1994-15)[[246]](#cite_note-Kevin_M._Gorey_and_Donald_R._Leslie_1997_pp391–398-246) A literature review of 23 studies found rates of 3% to 37% for males and 8% to 71% for females, which produced an average of 17% for boys and 28% for girls,[[247]](#cite_note-Rind-247) while a statistical analysis based on 16 cross-sectional studies estimated the rate to be 7.2% for males and 14.5% for females.[[246]](#cite_note-Kevin_M._Gorey_and_Donald_R._Leslie_1997_pp391–398-246) The [US Department of Health and Human Services](/wiki/US_Department_of_Health_and_Human_Services "US Department of Health and Human Services") reported 83,600 substantiated reports of sexually abused children in 2005,[[248]](#cite_note-248)[[249]](#cite_note-249) while state-level child protective services reported 63,527 sexual abuse incidents in 2010.[[250]](#cite_note-250) According to Emily M. Douglas and [David Finkelhor](/wiki/David_Finkelhor "David Finkelhor"), "Several national studies have found that [black](/wiki/African_American "African American") and [white](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_Whites "Non-Hispanic Whites") children experienced near-equal levels of sexual abuse. Other studies, however, have found that both blacks and [Latinos](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") have an increased risk for sexual victimization".[[251]](#cite_note-251)[[252]](#cite_note-252) Reports by the [Centers for Disease Control and Prevention](/wiki/Centers_for_Disease_Control_and_Prevention "Centers for Disease Control and Prevention") reveal that about 1 in 4 girls and 1 in 20 boys in the United States experience child sexual abuse.[[253]](#cite_note-ff-253)
Surveys have shown that one fifth to one third of all women reported some sort of childhood sexual experience with a male adult.[[254]](#cite_note-Herman-254)
A study by Lawson & Chaffin indicated that many children who were sexually abused were "identified solely by a physical complaint that was later diagnosed as a venereal disease ... Only 43% of the children who were diagnosed with venereal disease made a verbal disclosure of sexual abuse during the initial interview."[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuehnle19967-255) It has been found in the epidemiological literature on CSA that there is no identifiable demographic or family characteristic of a child that can be used to bar the prospect that a child has been sexually abused.[[244]](#cite_note-Finklehor-epid-244)

[Child marriage](/wiki/Child_marriage "Child marriage") is often considered to be another form of child sexual abuse.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) Over 200,000 marriages involving minors were allowed between 2000 and 2015 in the US. These marriages were most often between an adult male and female minor.[[256]](#cite_note-:2-256) [Child marriage in the United States](/wiki/Child_marriage_in_the_United_States "Child marriage in the United States") is allowed in the majority of states as long as parental consent or judicial approval (typically for pregnancy) is given.[[256]](#cite_note-:2-256)

In US schools, according to the [United States Department of Education](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Education "United States Department of Education"),[[257]](#cite_note-shakeshaft2004-257) "nearly 9.6% of students are targets of educator sexual misconduct sometime during their school career." In studies of student sex abuse by male and female educators, male students were reported as targets in ranges from 23% to 44%.[[257]](#cite_note-shakeshaft2004-257) In U.S. school settings same-sex (female and male) sexual misconduct against students by educators "ranges from 18 to 28% of reported cases, depending on the study"[[258]](#cite_note-258) An American survey found that children sexually abused by relatives were much more likely to be affiliated with Protestantism, while persons sexually abused by nonrelatives were affiliated with liberal denominations or irreligious.[[259]](#cite_note-Ebaugh_2006_p._252-259)

Significant underreporting of sexual abuse of boys by both women and men is believed to occur due to sex stereotyping, social denial, the minimization of male victimization, and the relative lack of research on sexual abuse of boys.[[260]](#cite_note-260) Sexual victimization of boys by their mothers or other female relatives is particularly rarely researched or reported. Sexual abuse of girls by their mothers, and other related and/or unrelated adult females is beginning to be researched and reported despite the highly taboo nature of female–female child sex abuse. In studies where students are asked about sex offenses, they report higher levels of female sex offenders than found in adult reports.[[261]](#cite_note-261) This underreporting has been attributed to cultural denial of female-perpetrated child sex abuse,[[262]](#cite_note-262) because "males have been socialized to believe they should be flattered or appreciative of sexual interest from a female."[[156]](#cite_note-Shakeshaft25-156) Journalist Cathy Young writes that [under-reporting](/wiki/Under-reporting "Under-reporting") is contributed to by the difficulty of people, including jurors, in seeing a male as a "true victim".[[263]](#cite_note-263)

### Europe

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), reported child sex abuse has increased, but this may be due to greater willingness to report. Police need more resources to deal with it. Also parents and schools need to give children and adolescents regular advice about how to spot abuse and about the need to report abuse. Software providers are urged to do more to police their environment and make it safe for children.[[264]](#cite_note-264)
In the UK, a 2010 study estimated prevalence at about 5% for boys and 18% for girls[[265]](#cite_note-265) (not dissimilar to a 1985 study that estimated about 8% for boys and 12% for girls[[266]](#cite_note-Baker-266)). More than 23,000 incidents were recorded by the UK police between 2009 and 2010. Girls were six times more likely to be assaulted than boys with 86% of attacks taking place against them.[[267]](#cite_note-267)[[268]](#cite_note-268) [Barnardo's](/wiki/Barnardo%27s "Barnardo's") [charity](/wiki/Charitable_organization "Charitable organization") estimates that two thirds of victims in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") are girls and one third are boys. Barnardo's is concerned that boy victims may be overlooked.[[269]](#cite_note-269)

A 1992 survey studying father-daughter incest in [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") reported that of the 9,000 15-year-old high school girls who filled out the questionnaires, of the girls living with their biological fathers, 0.2% reported father-daughter incest experiences; of the girls living with a stepfather, 3.7% reported sexual experiences with him. The reported counts included only father-daughter incest and did not include prevalence of other forms of child sexual abuse. The survey summary stated, "the feelings of the girls about their incestual experiences are overwhelmingly negative."[[270]](#cite_note-270)

### In pre-industrial societies

[Cross-cultural studies](/wiki/Cross-cultural_studies "Cross-cultural studies") have reported that sexual relations between men and pubescent girls were sometimes performed for functional reasons in [pre-industrial societies](/wiki/Pre-industrial_society "Pre-industrial society").[[271]](#cite_note-271) Other accounts of sexual relations between adults and minors have also been registered. A 1951 research document reports [Siwan](/wiki/Siwi_people "Siwi people") men engaging with anal intercourse with boys. The report also stated that, among [Aranda aborigines](/wiki/Arrernte_people "Arrernte people"), "pederasty [between a man and a boy between the ages of ten and twelve] is a recognized custom". An 18th-century report by [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") reported an act of copulation between a man and a female estimated to be 11 or 12 years old in a public street "without the least sense of it being indecent or improper". In some Oceanic societies, adult men have been reported to have sexual contact with prepubertal females. A 19th-century document by missionary John Muggeridge Orsmond reads that "in all [Tahitians](/wiki/Tahitians "Tahitians") as well as officers who come in ships there is a cry for little girls". Other instances of adult-child sexual behaviors have been reported in the [Marquesas Islands](/wiki/Marquesas_Islands "Marquesas Islands"), [Polynesia](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia"), [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea") and in [Kaluli](/wiki/Kaluli_people "Kaluli people") societies.[[272]](#cite_note-272)

Accounts of sexual intercourse between children and adults have also been reported in [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Pederasty_in_ancient_Greece "Pederasty in ancient Greece") and Rome.[[273]](#cite_note-273)[[274]](#cite_note-274)

## International law

Main article: [Laws regarding child sexual abuse](/wiki/Laws_regarding_child_sexual_abuse "Laws regarding child sexual abuse")

Child sexual abuse is outlawed nearly everywhere in the world, generally with severe criminal penalties, including in some jurisdictions, [life imprisonment](/wiki/Life_sentence "Life sentence") or [capital punishment](/wiki/Capital_punishment "Capital punishment").[[275]](#cite_note-Levesque-275)[[276]](#cite_note-UNchildren-276) An adult's sexual intercourse with someone below the legal [age of consent](/wiki/Age_of_consent "Age of consent") is defined as [statutory rape](/wiki/Statutory_rape "Statutory rape"),[[277]](#cite_note-277) based on the principle that a child is not capable of consent and that any apparent consent by a child is not considered to be [legal consent](/wiki/Legal_consent#Sex "Legal consent").

The United Nations [Convention on the Rights of the Child](/wiki/Convention_on_the_Rights_of_the_Child "Convention on the Rights of the Child") (CRC) is an international [treaty](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty") that legally obliges states to protect children's rights. Articles 34 and 35 of the CRC require states to protect children from all forms of sexual exploitation and sexual abuse. This includes outlawing the coercion of a child to perform sexual activity, the [prostitution of children](/wiki/Prostitution_of_children "Prostitution of children"), and the [exploitation of children in creating pornography](/wiki/Child_pornography "Child pornography"). States are also required to prevent the abduction, sale, or trafficking of children.[[278]](#cite_note-278) As of November 2008, 193 countries are bound by the CRC,[[279]](#cite_note-untreaty-279) including every member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") except the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan").[[280]](#cite_note-280)[[281]](#cite_note-281)

The Council of Europe has adopted the [Council of Europe Convention on the Protection of Children against Sexual Exploitation and Sexual Abuse](/wiki/Council_of_Europe_Convention_on_the_Protection_of_Children_against_Sexual_Exploitation_and_Sexual_Abuse "Council of Europe Convention on the Protection of Children against Sexual Exploitation and Sexual Abuse") in order to prohibit child sexual abuse that occurs within home or family.

In the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), child sexual abuse is subject to a [directive](/wiki/Directive_(European_Union) "Directive (European Union)").[[282]](#cite_note-282) This directive deals with several forms of sexual abuse of children, especially [commercial sexual exploitation of children](/wiki/Commercial_sexual_exploitation_of_children "Commercial sexual exploitation of children").

## Challenges in enforcement

Although efforts for the enforcement of child protection laws have increased internationally, in countries with inadequate legal systems or where corruption runs rampant, the implementation of these laws has been difficult.[[283]](#cite_note-283) There are several cases where law enforcement agencies lack the manpower and resources to efficiently resolve CSA crimes, which leads to reduced rates of prosecution and [conviction](/wiki/Conviction "Conviction").[[284]](#cite_note-284) Furthermore, with the addition of ever-changing technology and new information, the tension placed on law enforcement agencies to effectively pursue, and arrest offenders has increased. To effectively address these challenges, cooperation among government agencies, [international organizations](/wiki/International_organization "International organization"), and [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement "Law enforcement") must increase, along with the development of a new legal agenda to improve the training of law enforcement on how to properly handle CSA.[[285]](#cite_note-285)[[286]](#cite_note-286)

## Research

Child sexual abuse has gained public attention since the 1970s and has become one of the most high-profile crimes. While sexual use of children by adults has been present throughout history, public interest in prevention has tended to fluctuate.[[287]](#cite_note-:0-287) Initially, concern centered around children under the age of ten, but over time, advocates have attracted attention toward the sexual abuse of children between the ages of 11 and 17.[[287]](#cite_note-:0-287) Up until the 1930s, the psychological impact of sexual abuse was not emphasized, instead emphasis was placed on the physical harm or the child's reputation.[[287]](#cite_note-:0-287) Widespread public awareness of children's sexual abuse did not occur until the 1970s in the West.[[288]](#cite_note-288)

### Early writings

The first published work dedicated specifically to child sexual abuse appeared in France in 1857: *Medical-Legal Studies of Sexual Assault* *(Etude Médico-Légale sur les Attentats aux Mœurs)*, by [Auguste Ambroise Tardieu](/wiki/Auguste_Ambroise_Tardieu "Auguste Ambroise Tardieu"), the noted French pathologist and pioneer of [forensic medicine](/wiki/Forensic_medicine "Forensic medicine").[[289]](#cite_note-289)

## In society

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Globe icon. | The examples and perspective in this section **deal primarily with the United States and do not represent a [worldwide view](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Countering_systemic_bias "Wikipedia:WikiProject Countering systemic bias") of the subject**. You may [improve this section](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Child_sexual_abuse&action=edit), discuss the issue on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Child_sexual_abuse "Talk:Child sexual abuse"), or create a new section, as appropriate. *(October 2015)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Child sexual abuse became a public issue in the 1970s and 1980s. Prior to this point in time, sexual abuse remained rather secretive and socially unspeakable.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Studies on child molestation were nonexistent until the 1920s and the first national estimate of the number of child sexual abuse cases was published in 1948. By 1968 44 out of 50 U.S. states had enacted mandatory laws that required physicians to report cases of suspicious child abuse. Legal action began to become more prevalent in the 1970s with the enactment of the [Child Abuse Prevention and Treatment Act](/wiki/Child_Abuse_Prevention_and_Treatment_Act "Child Abuse Prevention and Treatment Act") in 1974 in conjunction with the creation of the National Center for Child Abuse and Neglect. Since the creation of the Child Abuse and Treatment Act, reported child abuse cases have increased dramatically. Finally, the [National Abuse Coalition](/w/index.php?title=National_Abuse_Coalition&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Abuse Coalition (page does not exist)") was created in 1979 to create pressure in congress to create more sexual abuse laws.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Second wave feminism](/wiki/Second_wave_feminism "Second wave feminism") brought greater awareness of child sexual abuse and [violence against women](/wiki/Violence_against_women "Violence against women"), and made them public, political issues.[[290]](#cite_note-290)[[291]](#cite_note-291) [Judith Lewis Herman](/wiki/Judith_Lewis_Herman "Judith Lewis Herman"), Harvard professor of psychiatry, wrote the first book ever on father-daughter incest when she discovered during her medical residency that a large number of the women she was seeing had been victims of father-daughter incest. Herman notes that her approach to her clinical experience grew out of her involvement in the civil rights movement.[[292]](#cite_note-UCB-292) Her second book *Trauma and Recovery* coined the term [complex post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Complex_post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Complex post-traumatic stress disorder") and included child sexual abuse as a cause.[[293]](#cite_note-Herman1997-293)

In 1986, Congress passed the [Child Abuse Victims' Rights Act](/w/index.php?title=Child_Abuse_Victims%27_Rights_Act&action=edit&redlink=1 "Child Abuse Victims' Rights Act (page does not exist)"), giving children a civil claim in sexual abuse cases. The number of laws created in the 1980s and 1990s began to create greater prosecution and detection of child sexual abusers. During the 1970s a large transition began in the legislature related to child sexual abuse. [Megan's Law](/wiki/Megan%27s_Law "Megan's Law") which was enacted in 1996 gives the public access to knowledge of sex offenders nationwide.[[294]](#cite_note-wright-294)

Anne Hastings described these changes in attitudes towards child sexual abuse as "the beginning of one of history's largest social revolutions."[[295]](#cite_note-295)

According to John Jay College of Criminal Justice professor B.J. Cling:

> By the early 21st century, the issue of child sexual abuse has become a legitimate focus of professional attention, while increasingly separated from second wave feminism ... As child sexual abuse becomes absorbed into the larger field of interpersonal trauma studies, child sexual abuse studies and intervention strategies have become degendered and largely unaware of their political origins in modern feminism and other vibrant political movements of the 1970s. One may hope that unlike in the past, this rediscovery of child sexual abuse that began in the 70s will not again be followed by collective amnesia. The institutionalization of child maltreatment interventions in federally funded centers, national and international societies, and a host of research studies (in which the United States continues to lead the world) offers grounds for cautious optimism. Nevertheless, as Judith Herman argues cogently, 'The systematic study of psychological trauma ... depends on the support of a political movement.'[[296]](#cite_note-296)

### Media reporting and its quality

Media reporting plays a crucial role in tackling the world-wide problem of child sexual abuse because it puts the issue on the public and political agenda.[[297]](#cite_note-297) Media reporting can even contribute to the exposure and criminal investigation of sexual abuse cases in institutions. One notorious example is the *[Boston Globe](/wiki/The_Boston_Globe "The Boston Globe")* coverage of the [sex abuse scandal in the Catholic Archdiocese of Boston](/wiki/Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Boston_sex_abuse_scandal "Catholic Archdiocese of Boston sex abuse scandal") for which the newspaper received a [Pulitzer Prize for Public Service](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Public_Service "Pulitzer Prize for Public Service") in 2003. Another award-winning example is the *[Indianapolis Star](/wiki/The_Indianapolis_Star "The Indianapolis Star")* coverage of the [USA Gymnastics sex abuse scandal](/wiki/USA_Gymnastics_sex_abuse_scandal "USA Gymnastics sex abuse scandal") in 2016. Media reporting can be very beneficial by giving survivors a voice and informing the public.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Media reporting can also violate the rights of abuse survivors and disseminate misleading and harmful messages. Content analyses of news reporting have revealed several quality issues such as a focus on sensationalized individual cases (so-called episodic framing) and neglect of thematic framing in the sense of contextualizing individual cases and pointing to the systematic problems that enable child sexual abuse.[[298]](#cite_note-298)[[299]](#cite_note-299) When media reporting on child sexual abuse is investigated, usual methodological approaches are the media [content analysis](/wiki/Content_analysis "Content analysis") and the media quality analysis.[[300]](#cite_note-300) Here it is important to not only analyze text but also documentary and stock photos commonly used in media that report about child sexual abuse. Research shows that myths and stereotypes about child sexual abuse are disseminated through text and images alike.[[301]](#cite_note-301) Several checklists and guidelines for journalist have been published by violence prevention and journalism organizations to help improve the quality of news reporting on child sexual abuse.[[302]](#cite_note-302)[[303]](#cite_note-303)

### Civil lawsuits

In the United States, growing awareness of child sexual abuse has sparked an increasing number of civil [lawsuits](/wiki/Lawsuits "Lawsuits") for [monetary damages](/wiki/Monetary_damages "Monetary damages") stemming from such incidents. Increased awareness of child sexual abuse has encouraged more victims to come forward, whereas in the past victims often kept their abuse secret. Some states have enacted specific [laws](/wiki/Laws "Laws") lengthening the applicable [statutes of limitations](/wiki/Statutes_of_limitations "Statutes of limitations") so as to allow victims of child sexual abuse to file suit sometimes years after they have reached the age of majority.[[304]](#cite_note-304) Such lawsuits can be brought where a person or entity, such as a school, church or youth organization, or daycare was charged with supervising the child but failed to do so with child sexual abuse resulting, making the individual or institution liable. In the [Catholic sex abuse cases](/wiki/Catholic_sex_abuse_cases "Catholic sex abuse cases"), the various [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") Diocese in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") have paid out approximately $1 billion settling hundreds of such lawsuits since the early 1990s. There have also been lawsuits involving the [American religious right](/wiki/American_religious_right "American religious right"). Crimes have allegedly gone unreported and victims were pressured into silence.[[305]](#cite_note-305)
As lawsuits can involve demanding procedures, there is a concern that [children](/wiki/Children "Children") or adults who file suit will be re-victimized by defendants through the [legal process](/wiki/Legal_process "Legal process"), much as [rape](/wiki/Rape "Rape") victims can be re-victimized by the accused in [criminal](/wiki/Criminal "Criminal") rape trials. The child sexual abuse plaintiff's attorney [Thomas A. Cifarelli](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_A._Cifarelli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Thomas A. Cifarelli (page does not exist)") has written that children involved in the legal system, particularly victims of sexual abuse and molestation, should be afforded certain procedural safeguards to protect them from harassment during the legal process.[[306]](#cite_note-306)

In June 2008 in [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia"), the issue of teacher-student sexual abuse and sexual assault was brought to the attention of the High Court of Zambia where a landmark case decision, with presiding Judge Philip Musonda, awarded $45 million Zambian kwacha (US$13,000) to the plaintiff, a 13-year-old girl for sexual abuse and rape by her school teacher. This claim was brought against her teacher as a "person of authority" who, as Judge Musonda stated, "had a moral superiority (responsibility) over his students" at the time.[[307]](#cite_note-307)

A 2000 World Health Organization – Geneva report, "World Report on Violence and Health (Chap 6 – Sexual Violence)" states, "Action in schools is vital for reducing sexual and other forms of violence. In many countries a sexual relation between a teacher and a pupil is not a serious disciplinary offence and policies on sexual harassment in schools either do not exist or are not implemented. In recent years, though, some countries have introduced laws prohibiting sexual relations between teachers and pupils. Such measures are important in helping eradicate sexual harassment in schools. At the same time, a wider range of actions is also needed, including changes to teacher training and recruitment and reforms of curricula, so as to transform gender relations in schools."[[308]](#cite_note-308)

"Parties shall take all appropriate legislative, administrative, social and educational measures to protect the child from all forms of physical or mental violence, injury or abuse, neglect or negligent treatment, maltreatment or exploitation, including sexual abuse ... States Parties undertake to protect the child from all forms of sexual exploitation and sexual abuse. For these purposes, States Parties shall in particular take all appropriate national, bilateral and multilateral measures to prevent: (a) The inducement or coercion of a child to engage in any unlawful sexual activity; (b) The exploitative use of children in prostitution or other unlawful sexual practices; (c) The exploitative use of children in pornographic performances and materials."
